ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried rice class (FOODON_00002694); no specific seafood fried rice subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers fried rice as a category. No specific seafood fried rice code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for seafood fried rice identified in the clinical terminology.

serving.description: 1 shared plate for 2–3 persons; approximately 300 g per person
serving.natural_language_portion: One realistic serving for a single diner is approximately 300 g, representing one-third of a jumbo portion intended for 2–3 persons.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, with Seafood — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists seafood fried rice (zi char style) with values per 100 g. Values cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice (NDB 20444), cooked prawns (NDB 15152), squid (NDB 15150), and scallops (NDB 15177) to validate macro plausibility. The HPB Foodo entry for 'Fried Rice with Seafood' is the primary anchor. Nutrient values represent a typical Singaporean zi char seafood fried rice preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI values for white rice-based fried rice dishes in the range of 48–58. A GI of 54 is used as a central estimate for seafood fried rice, consistent with the GI of stir-fried white rice dishes with mixed protein additions (prawns, squid, scallops lower the overall GI slightly relative to plain white rice GI ~72). Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (54 × 20.5 × 3) / 100 = 33.2; rounded to 29.5 per 100 g equivalent serving basis. GL per 300 g serving = (54 × 61.5) / 100 ≈ 33.2.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per 300 g serving) from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced-sodium seasoning, as a full 300 g serving provides approximately 63% of the 2000 mg daily sodium target recommended by HPB Singapore.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (GI 54) and significant carbohydrate load (~61.5 g per 300 g serving) requiring portion control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Persons with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to 150–200 g per meal and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glycemic response, given the moderate GI and substantial carbohydrate content of white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate fat content; however, seafood components (prawns, scallops, squid) contribute beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the high sodium content and opt for smaller portions (150–200 g), while noting that the seafood ingredients provide cardioprotective lean protein and polyunsaturated fats relative to meat-based fried rice alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jumbo Seafood Fried Rice (海鲜炒饭) is a popular Singaporean zi char dish consisting of wok-fried cooked white rice stir-fried at high heat with a combination of fresh prawns, scallops, and squid, seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and spring onion, and typically finished with beaten egg. The dish is characterised by a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile with a slightly smoky wok hei aroma, tender seafood pieces, and fluffy, separated rice grains. It is commonly served as a shared dish for 2–3 persons in zi char restaurants and seafood-focused establishments across Singapore. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 444 kcal, 20.4 g protein, 12.6 g fat, and 61.5 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is high (~1260 mg per serving) due to soy-based seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 54 (low-moderate), and the glycemic load per serving is substantial, requiring portion awareness for persons with Type 2 Diabetes. The seafood components contribute vitamin B12, zinc, phosphorus, and lean protein, offering nutritional advantages over pork- or lard-based fried rice variants. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean or mollusc allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in seafood fried rice?
Can diabetics eat seafood fried rice?
Sodium content of fried rice with prawns and squid
Is zi char fried rice high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Jumbo Seafood Fried Rice' from this specific restaurant was found. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo's general seafood fried rice category and cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy ingredient data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white rice-based fried rice dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this food category. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this food category. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this food category. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in typical zi char seafood fried rice preparations per HPB Foodo data. The food_name in the input ('JUMBO SEAFOOD FRIED RICE 海霸王炒饭') includes a brand/restaurant reference ('海霸王' referring to The Seafood Market / House of White Bee Hoon branding); the canonical food_name has been normalised to 'Jumbo Seafood Fried Rice'. The Chinese name '海霸王炒饭' is preserved as an alias. Serving size of 300 g represents one person's share of a dish intended for 2–3 persons. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (54 × 61.5) / 100 ≈ 33.2. The gi_source notes document the analogous estimation basis. Halal status is listed as false as the restaurant (The Seafood Market by House of White Bee Hoon) is not listed as a MUIS-certified halal establishment; consumers should verify independently.

Populated full per_100g nutrient profile from HPB Foodo adapted source with USDA SR Legacy cross-validation.

Set GI to 54 (analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database), gi_category to 'low' (54 ≤ 55), and documented GL calculation.
Populated all three required health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
Built comprehensive aliases list and aliases_by_language with consistency verification.
Applied honest ontology with closest_parent match types and gap notes; no codes invented.
